evidence: V: 'So the demo is like human data. Like it's a human data oriented demo.' - Positioning crystallized: Careerspan sells human data, not software.
Decision made: 'Careerspan positioned as human data company, not AI company or recruiting tool'

Logan: 'Boom, mic drop for the hiring people. This is opted in candidate data... as things heat up with publicly scraped data, this is a really valuable strategy for you guys.' - Eightfold lawsuit context transformed into sales hook.

Logan: 'Can we make like a 30 second look at the information that we capture via Careerspan? Because if that's in an email... they don't have to wrap their heads around what it is we're describing. It's just like oh shit, I want that information.'
Logan: 'It's like kind of a baller move to put the lead list of workforce boards in. That's kind of like just giving value up front.'

Logan: 'The problem with our existing materials is that it takes a fuck ton of word space to explain what it is that we do and why it's relevant.' - This frustration became the creative constraint that drove the video solution.
Logan: 'I'm thinking a deck is not as impactful as watching it in action.'
V: 'Yeah, I don't think we should submit the deck.' - Decision point that shaped the entire collateral strategy.
V suggested building a 'David Spiegel bot' that could provide career coaching advice based on David's methodology.
David: 'I would want to instruct the AI to say like generally read my LinkedIn, read their LinkedIn, find the commonality and then message in this order with this amount of brevity.'

David outlined system requirements: David's background/methods + Candidate's background (LinkedIn, CareerSpan answers) + Target's context (their LinkedIn) + Candidate's goal = appropriate messaging using David's principles.

David: 'Where I see people end up needing the most help after they learn from me, is getting stuck on what to message. And it's not just what to initially message for cold outreach, it's like what to message for warm outreach first, what to message for cold outreach first, how to respond.'

V identified that Zo has a communication problem: technical users don't need it (they can run their own servers), while non-technical users don't understand the value proposition of a personal AI server.
The sweet spot is users smart enough to use ChatGPT Projects and custom GPTs but who hit limitations due to file size constraints and lack of persistent context.
David noted this is exactly where he's stuck—he has a ChatGPT project with his slides and transcripts but needs more capacity for mixed media storage across time.
David will send an email introducing Ben Erez to the Zo team. Ben is interested in working with founders and exploring AI.

source_display: Software as File Bundle Mental Model
evidence: V explained the mental model of software as 'a bundle of files and instructions' organized to create a particular effect.
V shared deepwiki.com, a tool that converts GitHub repositories into Wikipedia-style documentation. David: 'That's pretty cool. It explains much more clearly what all is possible in N5OS.'
V and Logan conducted a focused 'collateral blitz' session to develop outreach materials for Careerspan's sales pipeline.
Two distinct video/message versions: 1) Sourcing/Matching/External Hiring — for recruiting teams, 2) Internal Mobility — for enterprise HR/L&D
